payload.author: Juan Lentino
payload.content:
A meaningful share of the global music royalty pool ends up in the wrong place. The exact number depends on which collection society you ask, which territory, which year. Conservative industry estimates put it in the hundreds of millions annually in the United States alone, with billions sitting in unallocated pools globally.

This pool has a name: the black box. Money that was collected from someone who used a song, but couldn't be paid out to the rights holder because the system couldn't figure out who the rights holder was.

The reflexive explanation is that the music rights system is corrupt or broken. That isn't quite right. The system isn't broken. The metadata is.

What actually happens

When a song gets used (streamed, broadcast, performed, sampled), a payment gets generated. To route that payment to the right person, the collection society has to match the use to a song, the song to a writer, the writer to a publisher, and the publisher to a payee. Every step depends on metadata.

If the metadata is clean, the match happens automatically and the money flows. If the metadata is messy, the match fails, and the money sits in suspense until someone manually reconciles it. If the manual reconciliation also fails, the money stays in the black box. After a few years, depending on the territory, it gets redistributed to the catalog at large or to other rights holders entirely.

Most industry effort goes into cleaning up the metadata downstream. Reconciliation tools, ISWC matching services, AI-powered title-matching algorithms. These help, marginally. They don't solve the problem because they're working downstream of where the problem actually starts.

Where collected music royalties end up
A horizontal bar divided into four segments showing the distribution of collected music royalties: matched and paid, matched late, black box, and misallocated. The first segment is the largest, but a visible leakage area covers the remaining three categories. Proportions are illustrative.

WHERE COLLECTED ROYALTIES END UP

leakage

Matched and paid

Matched late

Black box

Misallocated

Illustrative. Proportions vary by territory, year, and collection society.

Why downstream cleanup can't catch up

The pipeline that generates the metadata problem is the same one I described in the C2PA piece. Every handoff between the artist, the producer, the distributor, the DSP, and the collection society is an opportunity for metadata to drop, get rewritten, or get associated with the wrong identifier.

By the time a song is generating royalties, its metadata has been touched by five or more parties, each of which had different assumptions about format, identifier system, and which fields matter. Reconciling that downstream is a forensic exercise.

Provenance doesn't try to reconstruct the chain after the fact. It signs the chain at creation and carries it through. That's the structural difference. Metadata cleanup is archaeology. Provenance is title insurance.

What changes with provenance

A provenance system doesn't eliminate black box money on day one. It eliminates the cause that creates new black box money on day two and beyond. Each work that enters the pipeline with verified authorship arrives at the collection society with an unbroken chain back to the human who made it. The match doesn't require reconciliation because the match was established at creation.

Over time, the black box stops growing for new works. Existing catalog still requires the cleanup tools and the manual reconciliation. The economic case for the system is the difference between those two curves.

This is why provenance is a label and rights holder problem first, and an artist problem second. The black box is real money sitting somewhere it doesn't belong, and the longer the industry treats it as an inevitable cost of doing business, the more of that money walks out the door every year.
payload.title: Music's billion-dollar metadata problem
